dialogue.utterance: Unit 4 respond for a sick person at 518 Redacted, Richmond.
Hi, I'm Jack, an EMT with the Fire and Rescue. May I ask what brought you to call 911 today?
Hi, I'm the building manager. I noticed the patient on the floor near the apartment door. She's 98 and wasn't her normal self.
I see. Thank you for the information. Did she say any words or express what was wrong?
She just said "I'm okay" slowly and keeps saying today is Sunday when in fact it's Wednesday.
Let me check her alertness. Ma'am, can you open your eyes for us?
Hmmm... I think I can hear you.
Great, thank you. What's your name?
Helen.
What day is it today, Helen?
Sunday.
Interesting. What's the name of this city?
Not sure.
And what happened to bring you here today?
I was just sitting, thinking.
Can you squeeze my finger for me, Helen? Here's my right hand.
She just sat there for a second, but then started moving her fingers slowly and was cooperative when asked.
She has a GCS of 15-eye 4, verbal 5, motor 6-but is unoriented to time. When corrected about the day, she became argumentative.
No signs of trauma or airway distress.
Let's take her respiratory rate. She's breathing at 18 per minute, stable. Normal chest rise with equal expansion.
Oxygen saturation is 79% on room air. That's a little low.
Heart rate is 57, regular. Blood pressure is 152 over 84. Skin condition?
Skin is normal in temperature and tone. No cyanosis noted.
Helen, can you tell me if you're experiencing any pain or discomfort anywhere?
No. I don't feel anything.
Thank you. Could you tell us about your medical history, Helen?
She has a documented history of primary hypertension. No other medical conditions were noted.
Are you taking any medications currently?
No medications were found on her. She is disoriented and confused.
Thank you. Could you let me know your name again?
My name is Helen. I live in this apartment.
She claims to live in the apartment. Manager provided her daughter's and son-in-law's phone numbers. No insurance or ID found.
On a scale from 0 to 10, with 10 being the worst pain you've ever had, how would you grade any discomfort you may be feeling, Helen?
I don't have any pain.
We will check your pupils. Is that okay with you, Helen?
Pupils are equal, round, and reactive (PERRL), about 3mm. No signs of trauma to the head or neck.
Let's listen to your lungs. Can you take a deep breath for me?
Equal bilateral breath sounds, no wheezes or crackles. Chest rises and falls normally.
Heart sounds are regular. No murmurs, extra heart sounds, or signs of tachycardia noted.
Moving to your belly now, pressing gently. No signs of tenderness, rigidity, or distension.
Let me check your fingers and toes gently for mobility. Does this hurt?
No deformities noted. Skin appears intact with no wounds or abrasions. No discharge.
Pelvis is stable. No deformity, pain, bleeding, or discharge.
No Battle signs observed. No jugular venous distention.
Blood pressure is 144 over 80. Stable for the moment.
Heart rate is 58. It's slightly low but steady.
Respiratory rate is 18 and regular.
Oxygen saturation still low at 74%. Need continued monitoring.
Glucose is at 0; it's undetectable.
Initiating 4-lead ECG monitoring per protocol. Rhythm shows artifact.
12-lead ECG attempted but failed due to patient movement. IV start on the arm attempted also unsuccessful.
Activating medical - altered mental status protocol since patient is significantly disoriented and unable to state correct day or time.
Oxygen saturation remains below 80%. Glucose is undetectable. Continue monitoring.
Blood pressure has slightly decreased to 144/80. Heart rate remains steady at 58.
Patient still says she has no pain but appears disoriented about the day and events. Continues to deny trauma or injury today.
Still no signs of pain or distress. Pain reported as zero.
Tried to capture 12-lead ECG again but unsuccessful due to her movement. Protocol requirement to note.
Transport en route to VCU Health Systems for further evaluation and to address low oxygen levels and undetectable glucose.
Ready to move to stretcher. Some resistance but we will place her in a semi-Fowler's position for comfort and monitoring.
She is securely fastened. Straps and wings are in place. Monitoring for any changes en route.
ED at VCU has been alerted via phone. Report given with patient's vitals and low glucose and SpO2.
On the way to the hospital. Patient appears calm, though still reporting wrong time. Vitals stable but SpO2 remains very low.
Patient has been received at the ED. Draw sheet used to transition to the hospital bed.
ED nurse received report and accepted care. Patient placed in bed 29 with assistance.
ED nurse signed paper indicating acceptance and documentation. Daughter and son-in-law phone numbers are on file.
We verbally handed over all vitals, procedures, and patient status to the nurse for review and treatment.
Responder safety confirmed. No injuries or exposures. EMS team followed proper procedure.
Full, clean patient handover to hospital staff. Patient is stable, but low glucose and oxygen levels need medical attention.
No suspected illegal activity. EMS team handed over directly to nurse and care plan is ongoing.
No adverse events during patient care. All procedures were justified and followed protocol.
No IV placed due to patient movement and non-cooperation. All patient information provided accurately.
EMS documentation completed. Update EMS incident log and conclude transport with incident report submitted.
